era_context: The early Iron Age Mediterranean and Near East. The Neo-Assyrian Empire is consolidating regional dominance. The Greek Archaic period is in its early phases. Phoenician maritime networks dominate Mediterranean trade. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is in its final years before the traditional collapse date of 771 BCE. Egypt remains fragmented under the late 21st Dynasty.

events.title: Assyrian military campaigns in Mesopotamia and the Levant
events.description: The Assyrian Empire was consolidating its control over Mesopotamia and expanding into the Levant during the late 9th and early 8th centuries BCE. By 795 BCE, Assyrian military campaigns were ongoing, though specific events for this year are not well-attested in surviving sources. The broader context of Assyrian expansion under Shalmaneser III's successors set the stage for the later conquests of Sargon II (r. 722–705 BCE). Assyrian military organization and tactics were becoming increasingly sophisticated, with the development of siege warfare and the use of iron weapons.

sources.name: Assyrian royal inscriptions and annals (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Assyrian military expansion is well-established. Specific events for 795 BCE are not independently documented. This entry reflects the era context rather than a documented event.

events.title: Greek polis development and early city-state formation
events.description: By the late 9th and early 8th centuries BCE, the Greek polis (city-state) system was emerging from the remnants of the Mycenaean civilization. By 795 BCE, early forms of political organization were developing in city-states like Athens, Corinth, Sparta, and Thebes. These communities were small, autonomous, and based on a central urban settlement with surrounding agricultural land. The polis system would become the defining political structure of ancient Greece. Archaeological evidence from this period shows the development of early temples, public spaces, and fortifications.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Greek settlements (pottery, architecture, fortifications)
Historiographic consensus on Greek Archaic period

events.certainty_note: The emergence of the polis system during the Archaic period (c. 800–480 BCE) is well-established archaeologically. Specific events for 795 BCE are not documented; this entry describes the broader political context.

events.title: Iron Age technological development in the Mediterranean
events.description: By 795 BCE, iron-working technology was becoming increasingly prevalent throughout the Mediterranean and Near East. Iron tools and weapons were gradually replacing bronze, though bronze continued to be used for luxury items and decorative purposes. The transition from the Bronze Age to the Iron Age was not sudden but gradual, occurring over several centuries. Iron-working allowed for the production of more durable and affordable tools, which had significant economic and social implications. Archaeological evidence from this period shows the increasing prevalence of iron artifacts in settlements throughout the Mediterranean and Near East.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from iron artifacts and metallurgical remains
Historiographic consensus on Iron Age technology

events.certainty_note: The gradual transition to iron-working technology during the Iron Age is well-established archaeologically. Specific events for 795 BCE are not documented; this entry describes the broader technological context.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Assyrian kings and campaigns in this period is debated, with some scholars proposing alternative dating schemes. The exact timeline of Greek polis development is also contested, with some scholars placing the emergence of the polis system earlier (c. 800 BCE) and others later (c. 750 BCE). The transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age technology is not a sharp boundary but a gradual process, and the dating of this transition varies by region. No disconfirming evidence specifically contradicts the events listed, but the dating precision is limited.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 795 BCE is minimal. No contemporary written records specifically document events of this year. The historical record relies on later compilations and archaeological evidence. Chronological uncertainty is significant, with margins of ±5–10 years common. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Mesopotamia and the Mediterranean; vast regions remain archaeologically silent or undocumented. The historical record for this period is shaped by the survival of certain types of evidence (inscriptions, monumental architecture, burials) and the absence of others (perishable materials, everyday objects). This creates a bias toward elite and monumental history.

graph_edges.note: Assyrian military consolidation of this era culminated in Sargon II's conquest of Israel in 722 BCE
Greek polis development continued through the traditional date of the first Olympic Games (776 BCE)
Iron Age technological development represents the continuation of the broader Iron Age transition that began around 1200 BCE
